title: How to Budget ₦100k in Lagos (Realistic 2026 Guide)
description: Living on ₦100k in Lagos? Here's a real budget for rent, transport, food, data, and savings. Plus a free template.

content.text: Let me be real with you.
₦100k in Lagos is not big money. If you live on the Island, that money can finish in two weeks. But if you're on the mainland, you can actually survive and even save small if you plan well.

I've been there. I shared a one-bedroom in Egbeda. I took BRT to work every day. I cooked beans and rice for days just to make salary last. And I learned one thing: the difference between suffering and smiling is not how much you earn. It's how you plan.

This guide is for anyone earning around ₦100k in Lagos. No fluff. No "stop buying coffee" rubbish we don't even drink that expensive stuff here. Just real numbers, real expenses, and a budget that actually works in Lagos in 2026.
Let's go.
1. First, understand Lagos reality
Before you write anything down, you need to accept three things about Lagos:

items: Transport will eat your money. A trip from Egbeda to Ikeja can cost ₦1,500 to ₦2,000 if you're taking buses and okada. That's ₦30k to ₦40k per month just to move around.
Rent is your biggest enemy. A decent self-contained on the mainland costs ₦350k to ₦500k per year. Break it down, that's ₦29k to ₦42k every month.

The "Lagos factor" is real. Everything is slightly more expensive water, food, even airtime. And every week, something unexpected pops up. A broken phone screen. Your landlord's "development levy." A friend's wedding.

content.text: But here's the good news: ₦100k is enough to cover your basics, save a little, and even enjoy one or two small treats if you budget intentionally.
2. Track your money for 7 days (no cheating)
You cannot budget what you don't measure.

For the next 7 days, write down every single expense. Not just the big ones. That ₦200 for pure water? Write it. That ₦500 for boli and fish? Write it. That ₦1,000 data subscription you forgot? Write it.
How to track:

items: Use a simple notebook
Or a free app like Money Manager or Spending Tracker (both work in Nigeria)
Or just use Google Sheets on your phone

content.text: After 7 days, you'll see the leaks. Most people are shocked to find they spend ₦10k to ₦15k on "small things" they don't even remember.
Real example from someone earning ₦95k in Lagos (2025):

items: Daily transport: ₦1,200 (bus + okada)
Small chops after work: ₦500 to ₦1,000 almost every day
Airtime and data: ₦6k but only used ₦4k worth
POS withdrawal charges: ₦500 to ₦1,000

content.text: That's over ₦50k gone without even paying rent or buying food.
So step one: **track everything** for one week. Then come back to this post.
3. The 50-30-20 rule Lagos style
You've probably heard of the 50-30-20 rule:

items: 50% for Needs
30% for Wants
20% for Savings

content.text: In Lagos, that rule needs a small adjustment because rent alone can eat 40% of your income.
Here's the Lagos version for ₦100k salary:
This is aggressive but doable. If you can't save ₦20k yet, start with ₦10k and grow from there.
4. Sample ₦100k monthly budgets (mainland Lagos)
These budgets assume you live on the **mainland** (Egbeda, Agege, Ojuelegba, Ikeja, Maryland, Oshodi, etc.) and your work transport costs ₦800 to ₦1,500 daily.
First option (a little flexible)

rows: Cook at home 90% of the time
For small flat (fridge, fan, TV, phone charging)
POS charges, airtime top-up, small repairs
One outing, small chops, cinema once a month

content.text: *Wait, that's ₦110k?* Yes. If your rent is ₦420k per year, that's ₦35k per month already. To stay within ₦100k, you need to reduce rent (share an apartment) or cut transport/food a bit.
Second option (if you earn exactly ₦100k – tighter)
This one is lean but realistic. You won't feel rich, but you also won't be borrowing at the end of the month.
5. How to actually survive on this budget
Budgeting is easy. Sticking to it is hard. Here's how to make it work in Lagos.
A. Rent smart

items: Share an apartment if you're single. A 2-bedroom in Egbeda or Agege costs ₦500k to ₦700k per year. Split with a friend = ₦250k to ₦350k each.
Avoid agent fee scams. Pay only for rent and one-year caution fee. Never pay for "inspection."
Live close to your work if possible. The money you save on transport will pay for slightly higher rent.

content.text: B. Cook like a Lagos pro

items: Bulk cooking on Sundays. Make a big pot of rice, beans, stew, or yam porridge. Store in the fridge (or cool in a pot if no fridge).
Buy foodstuffs from markets Mile 12, Oyingbo, Tejuosho, or your local market. Avoid supermarkets for staples.
Examples: 5kg rice: ₦7k-8k (lasts 2-3 weeks); Beans: ₦1,500-2,000 per paint bucket; Frozen chicken: ₦5k-6k for a whole chicken; Tomatoes, peppers, onions: buy in bulk from Oyingbo

content.text: C. Cut transport costs drastically

items: Use BRT or Lagos Rail when possible. BRT from Ikorodu to TBS is ₦500. Rail from Agege to Marina is cheap.
Walk short distances. If your bus stop is 15 minutes away, just walk. Save that ₦200 okada fare.
Get a monthly transport pass if your route has one (some BRT corridors).
Work from home if your job allows even 2 days a week.

content.text: D. Manage data and airtime

items: Buy data bundles don't use default rate. MTN's 20GB for ₦5k (30 days) is decent.
Use free WiFi at work, libraries, or some malls (but be careful with security).
Use WhatsApp calling over data instead of regular calls.

content.text: E. Electricity small changes, big savings

items: Use LED bulbs.
Unplug devices when not in use.
Run your fridge efficiently don't open it constantly, let food cool before storing.
Prepaid meter is a lifesaver you control exactly what you spend.

content.text: 6. Where to cut if you're struggling
If you try this budget and still run out before month end, cut these first:

rows: Bring a snack from home (groundnuts, biscuit, fruit)
Walk or take a bus (even if it means leaving 10 mins earlier)
Keep one network use dual SIM for best coverage
Once a week max, and stick to affordable places (mama put, not fancy cafes)
Withdraw larger amounts less often or use bank app transfers

content.text: Once you cut those, you can easily save an extra ₦5k to ₦10k per month.
7. Free budget template (for Lagos earners)
I created a **Google Sheets budget template** just for Lagos. It has:

items: Pre-filled categories for rent, food, transport, etc.
A column for your actual spending
Automatic totals and savings calculation

content.text: To get it: Drop a comment on this post saying "Send template" and I'll email it to you. Or use the form below if you want it immediately.

content.content: [Insert email capture form here: "Get my free Lagos Budget Calculator"]

content.text: The template will help you track every kobo without stress.
8. What about saving and investing?
Even on ₦100k, you can save and invest. Start small.
Emergency fund first

items: Goal: save 3 months of expenses (about ₦180k-200k)
Save ₦10k/month = 18-20 months (long but doable)
Save ₦20k/month = 9-10 months

content.text: Then invest

items: Thrift (Ajo/Esusu): Low risk, forces discipline. Join a trusted group or use an app like PiggyVest.
Crypto (safe): Don't gamble. Buy small amounts of Bitcoin or USDT on Binance or Quidax and hold.
Mini business: Use ₦20k-30k to buy and sell something (phone accessories, recharge cards, food items).

content.text: Never invest money you might need in the next 3 months.
9. Real talk will you feel rich on ₦100k?
No. You won't.
You won't buy a car. You won't rent on the Island. You won't eat at Eko Hotel.
But you **can**:

items: Pay your rent on time
Eat three decent meals a day
Afford data to learn new skills
Save a little every month
Avoid borrowing or running into debt

content.text: That's not "suffering." That's survival with dignity. And from there, you grow.
10. Your next move
This budget only works if you follow it.

items: Download the free template** (or use a notebook)
Track your spending for one week
Adjust the numbers to fit your real life
Review every Sunday what leaked? what worked?
Come back here in 30 days and tell me how it went

content.text: And if you want to increase your income (because budgeting alone won't make you rich), check out my next post: **Side Hustles to Add ₦50k in Lagos** – coming soon.
💬 Over to you
What's your biggest expense in Lagos? Rent? Transport? Food? Drop a comment. Let's help each other grind to green.
And if you found this useful, share it with one friend who earns around ₦100k. They'll thank you later.
